**Preston North End Football Club**, commonly referred to as **Preston**, **North End** or **PNE**, is a professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club in [Preston, Lancashire](/wiki/Preston,_Lancashire "Preston, Lancashire"), England. They currently play in the [EFL Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship"), the second level of the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system").

Originally a [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") club, PNE has been based at [Deepdale](/wiki/Deepdale "Deepdale") since 1875. The club first took up football in 1878 as a winter fitness activity, and decided to focus on it in May 1880, when the football club was officially founded. Deepdale is now football's oldest ground in terms of continuous use by a league club. Preston North End was a founder member of the [Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") in 1888. In the [1888–89 season](/wiki/1888%E2%80%9389_in_English_football "1888–89 in English football"), the team won both the [inaugural league championship](/wiki/List_of_English_football_champions "List of English football champions") and the [FA Cup](/wiki/1889_FA_Cup_final "1889 FA Cup final"), the latter without conceding a goal. They were the first team to achieve the "[Double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)")" in English football and, as they were unbeaten in all matches, are remembered as "[The Invincibles](/wiki/The_Invincibles_(English_football)#Preston_North_End "The Invincibles (English football)")". Preston won the league championship again in 1889–90 but their only major success since then has been their [1938 FA Cup final](/wiki/1938_FA_Cup_final "1938 FA Cup final") victory over [Huddersfield Town](/wiki/Huddersfield_Town_F.C. "Huddersfield Town F.C."). The club's most famous players have been [Sir Tom Finney](/wiki/Sir_Tom_Finney "Sir Tom Finney"), [Alan Kelly Sr.](/wiki/Alan_Kelly_Sr. "Alan Kelly Sr.") and [Bill Shankly](/wiki/Bill_Shankly "Bill Shankly"), who are all commemorated at Deepdale by stands named after them.

Until 1961, Preston were usually members of the [First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") but, having been relegated after the [1960–61 season](/wiki/1960%E2%80%9361_in_English_football "1960–61 in English football"), they have not yet returned to the top flight. They were first relegated to the [Third Division](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division") after the [1969–70 season](/wiki/1969%E2%80%9370_in_English_football "1969–70 in English football") and have spent 28 of the 49 seasons since 1970 in the bottom two divisions of the Football League, including a span of 19 seasons from 1981–1982 to 1999–2000. Preston has faced serious financial issues and was twice in danger of closure. The club was owned by businessman [Trevor Hemmings](/wiki/Trevor_Hemmings "Trevor Hemmings") until his death in October 2021 and has been in the EFL Championship since gaining promotion in 2015, with a highest finishing position since then of 7th ([2017–18](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Preston_North_End_F.C._season "2017–18 Preston North End F.C. season")).

Preston North End was founded in 1863, originally as a [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") club, and played their first matches at the Marsh near the [River Ribble](/wiki/River_Ribble "River Ribble") in the [Preston](/wiki/Preston,_Lancashire "Preston, Lancashire") suburb of [Ashton](/wiki/Ashton-on-Ribble "Ashton-on-Ribble"). Later that year, they switched to [Moor Park](/wiki/Moor_Park,_Preston "Moor Park, Preston") in the north of the town, calling themselves "North End" in recognition of the new location. On 21 January 1875, the club leased a field opposite Moor Park on the site of the current [Deepdale](/wiki/Deepdale "Deepdale") stadium, which has been its home ever since.[[1]](#cite_note-hist-1)

The club formed a [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") team in 1877 as a winter [fitness activity](/wiki/Physical_fitness "Physical fitness") but this was not a success and, a year later, they played their first game under the [rules of association football](/wiki/Laws_of_the_Game_(association_football) "Laws of the Game (association football)"). In May 1880, a proposal to fully adopt the association code was unanimously accepted and Preston North End Football Club was officially founded.[[1]](#cite_note-hist-1)

Preston became one of the first professional clubs by hiring players from Scotland. The players who came from Scotland to play in England in those days were known as the [Scotch Professors](/wiki/Scotch_Professors "Scotch Professors"). In the [1887-88 season](/wiki/1887%E2%80%9388_FA_Cup "1887–88 FA Cup"), they beat [Hyde](/wiki/Hyde_F.C._(1885) "Hyde F.C. (1885)") [26–0 in the first round of the FA Cup](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C._26%E2%80%930_Hyde_F.C. "Preston North End F.C. 26–0 Hyde F.C."), still a record winning margin in English first-class football. Scottish forward [Jimmy Ross](/wiki/Jimmy_Ross_(footballer_born_1866) "Jimmy Ross (footballer born 1866)") scored eight goals in the match before going on to score 19 goals in the competition that season, also still a record.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Preston_north_end_art.jpg/250px-Preston_north_end_art.jpg)](/wiki/File:Preston_north_end_art.jpg)

illustration of the 1888–89 Preston North End, the first Football League champions, subsequently doing '[The Double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)")

In [1888–89](/wiki/1888-89_in_English_football "1888-89 in English football"), Preston became the first league champions and the first winners of "[The Double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)")", becoming the only team to date to go throughout an entire season unbeaten in both the league and [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") – winning the FA Cup without conceding a goal.[[4]](#cite_note-Arsenal-4) The team did so with a majority of their team being made up of Scottish players (the [Scotch Professors](/wiki/Scotch_Professors "Scotch Professors")).[[5]](#cite_note-scotsman2-5) In a contribution to Paul Agnew's 1989 biography of [Tom Finney](/wiki/Tom_Finney "Tom Finney"), the player himself wrote: "The club has long been known as Proud Preston, and the Old Invincibles of the previous century set some incredible standards".[[6]](#cite_note-6) The author wrote elsewhere: "...and that team became immortalised as the 'Old Invincibles'".[[7]](#cite_note-7) Other sources call the team "The Invincibles" and both versions of the nickname have been used.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In his autobiography, Finney wrote: "The championship stayed with North End — by now tagged the Old Invincibles — the following year, but runners-up spot had to suffice for the next three seasons".[[9]](#cite_note-9) As Finney said, Preston were league champions again in 1889–90, but have not won the title since. In total, they have been league runners-up six times, including the three consecutive seasons from 1890-91 to 1892–93, and twice in the 1950s when Finney was playing. The club's last major trophy win was in the [1938 FA Cup Final](/wiki/1938_FA_Cup_Final "1938 FA Cup Final") when they defeated [Huddersfield Town](/wiki/Huddersfield_Town_F.C. "Huddersfield Town F.C.") 1–0 and the team included [Bill Shankly](/wiki/Bill_Shankly "Bill Shankly"), [Andy Beattie](/wiki/Andy_Beattie "Andy Beattie") and goalscorer [George Mutch](/wiki/George_Mutch "George Mutch").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Preston's most famous player, [Tom Finney](/wiki/Tom_Finney "Tom Finney"), joined the club as a teenager in 1938. His first team debut was delayed until 1946 by the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") but he played for Preston until he retired in 1960. He was nicknamed the "Preston Plumber" because of his local business. Finney remains the club's top goalscorer, with 187 goals from 433 appearances, and also scored 30 international goals for [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") in 76 appearances.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

A year after Finney's retirement, Preston were relegated to the [Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") and have not played in the top division since. They had a memorable [season in 1963–64](/wiki/1963%E2%80%9364_in_English_football "1963–64 in English football") when, managed by former player [Jimmy Milne](/wiki/Jimmy_Milne_(footballer,_born_1911) "Jimmy Milne (footballer, born 1911)"), they finished third in the Second Division and reached the [1964 FA Cup Final](/wiki/1964_FA_Cup_Final "1964 FA Cup Final") where they lost a thrilling match 3–2 to [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.").

Preston were first relegated to the [Third Division](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division") after the [1969–70 season](/wiki/1969%E2%80%9370_in_English_football "1969–70 in English football"). Although they won promotion again immediately, the team have spent 28 of the 49 seasons since 1970 in the bottom two divisions, including a span of 19 seasons from 1981-82 to 1999–2000. The club experienced a near-terminal decline in the 1980s which brought about the very real threat of closure, the nadir being the [1985–86 season](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_Football_League "1985–86 Football League") when they finished 23rd in the [Fourth Division](/wiki/Football_League_Fourth_Division "Football League Fourth Division") and had to seek re-election to the league.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/David_Moyes_%28201551591%29.jpg/250px-David_Moyes_%28201551591%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:David_Moyes_(201551591).jpg)

Under [David Moyes](/wiki/David_Moyes "David Moyes"), Preston were [Division Two](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") champions in 2000, and narrowly missed out on promotion to the Premier League the following season.

Under manager [John McGrath](/wiki/John_McGrath_(footballer,_born_1938) "John McGrath (footballer, born 1938)"), the team recovered and won promotion back to the Third Division only a year later but it was a false dawn as the team spent another three years in the bottom division from 1993 to 1996. The club finally began to recover and move forward after a takeover by heating manufacturer [Baxi](/wiki/Baxi "Baxi") in 1994 but their ownership ended in June 2002.[[12]](#cite_note-Col-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Baxi-13) The team's central defender [David Moyes](/wiki/David_Moyes "David Moyes"), then aged 34, began his managerial career when appointed by the Baxi-controlled board in February 1998. Moyes was successful and managed the team to the [third tier championship](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Football_League#Second_Division "1999–2000 Football League") in 2000. Preston reached the [2001 play-off final](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Football_League#First_Division_play-offs "2000–01 Football League") but were defeated by [Bolton Wanderers](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C."). In the [2005 play-off final](/wiki/2005_Football_League_play-offs "2005 Football League play-offs"), under Moyes' successor [Billy Davies](/wiki/Billy_Davies "Billy Davies"), Preston were beaten 1–0 by West Ham United.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Following the Baxi sell-off and the departure of Moyes to [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") in 2002, the team was established at second-tier level through the 2000s but more problems arose at the end of the decade with an [HM Revenue and Customs](/wiki/HM_Revenue_and_Customs "HM Revenue and Customs") winding-up order in 2010 and relegation to the third tier in 2011. The taxation issue was resolved by local businessman [Trevor Hemmings](/wiki/Trevor_Hemmings "Trevor Hemmings"), already a shareholder, who bought a controlling interest in June 2010.[[15]](#cite_note-Hemmings-15) The team were promoted again, via the play-offs, in 2015 and have remained in the [EFL Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship") since then, with a highest finishing position of 7th in the [2017–18 season](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Preston_North_End_F.C._season "2017–18 Preston North End F.C. season").

Preston North End reached the [2024–25 FA Cup quarter-finals](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_FA_Cup#Quarter-finals "2024–25 FA Cup") but they lost 3–0 against [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") at [Deepdale](/wiki/Deepdale "Deepdale").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Ground

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Preston_North_End_F.C.&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ground")]

Main article: [Deepdale](/wiki/Deepdale "Deepdale")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Deepdalecomplete.jpg/250px-Deepdalecomplete.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deepdalecomplete.jpg)

Deepdale stadium

Deepdale was the original cricket club's home from 1875 and has been a football venue from 1878. It is the world's oldest football ground in terms of continuous use by a club in a major league. The biggest attendance seen was 42,684 for a Division One clash with Arsenal in April 1938.

When Baxi took control, it embarked on an investment programme between 1996 and 2009 with the aim of upgrading Deepdale into a modern stadium. The old ground was demolished and rebuilt in four stages and the last of the new stands was opened in 2008. The stadium has a seated capacity of 23,404. The current pitch dimensions are 110 x 75 yards.[[17]](#cite_note-FGG-17)

Part of the Baxi-funded redevelopment was the original [National Football Museum](/wiki/National_Football_Museum "National Football Museum"), which opened at Deepdale in 2001 but was relocated to Manchester in 2012 after being closed for two years.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Statue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Preston_North_End_F.C.&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Statue")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Finney_statue_in_Preston.jpg/250px-Finney_statue_in_Preston.jpg)](/wiki/File:Finney_statue_in_Preston.jpg)

*The Splash* commemorates Preston legend [Tom Finney](/wiki/Tom_Finney "Tom Finney").

Outside the Sir Tom Finney Stand is a statue of the famous player himself, which is known as "The Splash" or the "Tom Finney Splash". The statue, sculpted by [Peter Hodgkinson](/wiki/Peter_Hodgkinson_(sculptor) "Peter Hodgkinson (sculptor)") and unveiled in July 2004, was inspired by a famous photograph taken at the [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") versus Preston game in 1956, played at [Stamford Bridge](/wiki/Stamford_Bridge_(stadium) "Stamford Bridge (stadium)") in particularly wet conditions.[[17]](#cite_note-FGG-17)

### 1913 terrorist incident

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Preston_North_End_F.C.&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1913 terrorist incident")]

See also: [Suffragette bombing and arson campaign](/wiki/Suffragette_bombing_and_arson_campaign "Suffragette bombing and arson campaign")

An attempt was made to destroy the ground in 1913. As part of the [suffragette bombing and arson campaign](/wiki/Suffragette_bombing_and_arson_campaign "Suffragette bombing and arson campaign"), [suffragettes](/wiki/Suffragettes "Suffragettes") carried out a series of bombings and arson attacks nationwide during their campaign for women's suffrage.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In April 1913, suffragettes attempted to burn down Deepdale's grandstand but were foiled.[[20]](#cite_note-Kay-20) In the same year, suffragettes [succeeded in burning down](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C.#Stadium_destruction_and_move_to_North_London "Arsenal F.C.") [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_FC "Arsenal FC")'s then South London stadium, and also attempted to burn down [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers "Blackburn Rovers")' ground.[[20]](#cite_note-Kay-20) More traditionally male sports were targeted in order to protest against [male dominance](/wiki/Patriarchy "Patriarchy").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Preston_North_End_F.C.&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

See also: [Preston North End F.C. league record by opponent](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C._league_record_by_opponent "Preston North End F.C. league record by opponent")

Historically, Preston North End's main rivalry is with [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.") — the two clubs' grounds being seventeen miles apart — and the [West Lancashire derby](/wiki/West_Lancashire_derby "West Lancashire derby") between the two clubs has been contested 96 times across all four divisions of the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") and cup competitions since 1901.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Preston's other local rivals in the league over the years include [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C."), [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C."), [Bolton Wanderers](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C.") and [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_A.F.C. "Wigan Athletic A.F.C.").

## Club records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Preston_North_End_F.C.&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Club records")]

* Most Goals (Overall): [Tom Finney](/wiki/Tom_Finney "Tom Finney"), 210 goals from 473 appearances.
* Most League Goals: Tom Finney, 187 goals from 433 appearances.
* Most Appearances (Overall): [Alan Kelly, Sr.](/wiki/Alan_Kelly,_Sr. "Alan Kelly, Sr."), 511 appearances (between 1958 and 1973).
* Most League Goals in a Season: [Ted Harper](/wiki/Ted_Harper "Ted Harper"), **37** in [Football League Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division"), [1932–33](/wiki/1932-33_in_English_football "1932-33 in English football")[[28]](#cite_note-Milestones-28)
* Record Attendance: **42,684** v [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."), [Football League First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division"), 23 April 1938[[28]](#cite_note-Milestones-28)
* Record Cup Victory: **26–0** v [Hyde](/wiki/Hyde_F.C._(1885) "Hyde F.C. (1885)"), FA Cup, 15 October 1887[[28]](#cite_note-Milestones-28)
* Record League Victory: **10–0** v [Stoke](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C."), [Football League Division One](/wiki/Football_League_Division_One "Football League Division One"), 14 September 1889[[28]](#cite_note-Milestones-28)
* Record Defeat: **0–7** v [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C."), Division 1, 1 May 1948
* Transfer Record (Paid):  **£2,130,000** for [Milutin Osmajić](/wiki/Milutin_Osmaji%C4%87 "Milutin Osmajić") from [Cádiz CF](/wiki/C%C3%A1diz_CF "Cádiz CF"), August 2023[[29]](#cite_note-OsmajićSale-29)
* Transfer Record (Received): **£10,000,000** for [Jordan Hugill](/wiki/Jordan_Hugill "Jordan Hugill") to [West Ham Utd](/wiki/West_Ham_Utd "West Ham Utd"), January 2018[[30]](#cite_note-HugillSale-30)
* Youngest Ever Player: [Ethan Walker](/wiki/Ethan_Walker "Ethan Walker") 16 years and 156 days
* Oldest Ever Player: [Bob Kelly](/wiki/Bob_Kelly_(footballer) "Bob Kelly (footballer)") 41 years and two months[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Preston_North_End_F.C.&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Honours")]

In 1996, Preston's Third Division title made them the third club to have been champions of each of the top four professional leagues in English football. This feat was previously achieved by [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") in 1988, and [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C.") in 1992, and has since been achieved by [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.") and [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.") both in 2017.

**League**

* [First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") (level 1)[[32]](#cite_note-divisions-32)
  + Champions: [1888–89](/wiki/1888%E2%80%9389_Football_League "1888–89 Football League"), [1889–90](/wiki/1889%E2%80%9390_Football_League "1889–90 Football League")
  + Runners-up: [1890–91](/wiki/1890%E2%80%9391_Football_League "1890–91 Football League"), [1891–92](/wiki/1891%E2%80%9392_Football_League "1891–92 Football League"), [1892–93](/wiki/1892%E2%80%9393_Football_League "1892–93 Football League"), [1905–06](/wiki/1905%E2%80%9306_Football_League "1905–06 Football League"), [1952–53](/wiki/1952%E2%80%9353_Football_League "1952–53 Football League"), [1957–58](/wiki/1957%E2%80%9358_Football_League "1957–58 Football League")
* [Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") (level 2)[[32]](#cite_note-divisions-32)
  + Champions: [1903–04](/wiki/1903%E2%80%9304_Football_League "1903–04 Football League"), [1912–13](/wiki/1912%E2%80%9313_Football_League "1912–13 Football League"), [1950–51](/wiki/1950%E2%80%9351_Football_League "1950–51 Football League")
  + Runners-up: [1914–15](/wiki/1914%E2%80%9315_Football_League "1914–15 Football League"), [1933–34](/wiki/1933%E2%80%9334_Football_League "1933–34 Football League")
* [Third Division](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division") / [Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") / [League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") (level 3)[[32]](#cite_note-divisions-32)
  + Champions: [1970–71](/wiki/1970%E2%80%9371_Football_League "1970–71 Football League"), [1999–2000](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Football_League "1999–2000 Football League")
  + Play-off winners: [2015](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Football_League#League_One_play-offs "2014–15 Football League")
* [Fourth Division](/wiki/Football_League_Fourth_Division "Football League Fourth Division") / [Third Division](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division") (level 4)[[32]](#cite_note-divisions-32)
  + Champions: [1995–96](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_Football_League "1995–96 Football League")
  + Runners-up: [1986–87](/wiki/1986%E2%80%9387_Football_League "1986–87 Football League")

**Cup**

* [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup")
  + Winners: [1888–89](/wiki/1888%E2%80%9389_FA_Cup "1888–89 FA Cup"), [1937–38](/wiki/1937%E2%80%9338_FA_Cup "1937–38 FA Cup")
  + Runners-up: [1887–88](/wiki/1887%E2%80%9388_FA_Cup "1887–88 FA Cup"), [1921–22](/wiki/1921%E2%80%9322_FA_Cup "1921–22 FA Cup"), [1936–37](/wiki/1936%E2%80%9337_FA_Cup "1936–37 FA Cup"), [1953–54](/wiki/1953%E2%80%9354_FA_Cup "1953–54 FA Cup"), [1963–64](/wiki/1963%E2%80%9364_FA_Cup "1963–64 FA Cup")
* [FA Charity Shield](/wiki/FA_Community_Shield "FA Community Shield")
  + Runners-up: [1938](/wiki/1938_FA_Charity_Shield "1938 FA Charity Shield")
* [Football League War Cup](/wiki/Football_League_War_Cup "Football League War Cup")
  + Winners: [1940–41](/wiki/1940%E2%80%9341_in_English_football "1940–41 in English football")
* [Lancashire Senior Cup](/wiki/Lancashire_Senior_Cup "Lancashire Senior Cup")
  + Winners: 1886–87, 1892–93, 1894–95, 1899–1900, 1938–39, 1996–97

## Women's football
